translation: Ha, Meem
commentary:
حمٓ

This surah begins with the disconnected letters Ha Meem, one of the seven surahs in the Quran that opens with this powerful combination. These letters stand as a divine signature, reminding us that this Book comes from beyond human capacity.

translation: The revelation of the Book is from Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Wise
commentary:
تَنزِيلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ

The revelation - the sending down over time - of this Book is from Allah ﷻ, the Ultimate Authority, the One full of wisdom. The word تَنزِيل  emphasizes the gradual descent of the Quran, distinguishing it from the single revelation of previous scriptures. Allah ﷻ identifies Himself here as Al-Aziz, the One who cannot be overpowered, and Al-Hakeem, the Perfectly Wise. Every word in this Book comes from One who possesses absolute authority and perfect wisdom.

translation: Indeed, within the heavens and earth are signs for the believers
commentary:
إِنَّ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

Without doubt, in the skies and the earth there are many miraculous signs for true believers. Allah ﷻ begins directing our attention to His creation. The ayat - signs or miracles - are scattered throughout the heavens and earth for those willing to see them. But notice the condition: these signs are specifically لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (for the believers). This isn't because the signs themselves change, but because the heart that receives them makes all the difference.

translation: And in the creation of yourselves and what He disperses of moving creatures are signs for people who are certain [in faith]
commentary:
وَفِى خَلْقِكُمْ وَمَا يَبُثُّ مِن دَآبَّةٍ ءَايَـٰتٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ

And in your own creation, and in all the creatures that are spread across the earth, there are miraculous signs for a nation that seeks to be convinced. After pointing to the vast creation of the skies and earth, Allah ﷻ brings our attention even closer - to ourselves. The ayat aren't just "out there" in the cosmos; they're within our own bodies, in our own existence. Every living creature scattered across this planet carries within it evidence of divine design. These signs are for قَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ (a people who seek certainty), those who are genuinely searching for conviction in their hearts.

translation: And [in] the alternation of night and day and [in] what Allah sends down from the sky of provision and gives life thereby to the earth after its lifelessness and [in His] directing of the winds are signs for a people who reason
commentary:
وَٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ وَمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مِن رِّزْقٍ فَأَحْيَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا وَتَصْرِيفِ ٱلرِّيَـٰحِ ءَايَـٰتٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ

And in the alternation of night and day, and in what Allah ﷻ sent down from the sky in terms of provision - giving life to the earth after its death - and in the shifting of the winds, there are miraculous signs for a nation that seeks to understand.

The ayah continues building this case for reflection on creation. The daily cycle of night and day, something we witness every single day of our lives, carries profound meaning. Allah ﷻ describes water as رِزْق ( provision) from the sky, because essentially all of our sustenance traces back to water. The rain brings dead earth back to life, and even the winds serve a purpose - carrying pollen from place to place, moving clouds laden with rain, all part of an intricate system of provision.

These signs are for قَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (a people who use their intellect). Notice how each ayah addresses a different faculty: believers in ayah 3, those seeking certainty in ayah 4, and now those who use their minds. Allah ﷻ is calling on every dimension of human perception.

translation: These are the verses of Allah which We recite to you in truth. Then in what statement after Allah and His verses will they believe
commentary:
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱللَّهِ نَتْلُوهَا عَلَيْكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ فَبِأَىِّ حَدِيثٍۭ بَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ وَءَايَـٰتِهِۦ يُؤْمِنُونَ

Those are the ayat of Allah ﷻ - We are reciting them to you with truth and purpose. Then what speech, after Allah ﷻ and His miracles, are they going to believe in?

This ayah delivers a powerful conclusion to the evidence presented. Allah ﷻ says فَبِأَىِّ حَدِيثٍۭ بَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ (what narration after Allah). In Surah Al-Hadid, we learned that Allah ﷻ is both the Most Obvious and the Most Hidden. He's the Most Obvious because His creation screams of His existence - how could all of this be anything else, from anyone else? Yet He's also the Most Hidden because we don't see Him with our eyes. When Allah ﷻ calls on all of this creation as evidence, and then on top of that reveals the Quran itself, the question becomes unavoidable: what else could possibly be more convincing? What other proof are people waiting for?

translation: Woe to every sinful liar
commentary:
وَيْلٌ لِّكُلِّ أَفَّاكٍ أَثِيمٍ

The worst, most terrible form of destruction awaits every single one who fabricates vicious lies, who is constantly engrossed in sin.

The word أَفَّاك describes someone who habitually makes up lies, particularly scandalous accusations against others. Paired with أَثِيم , one constantly engrossed in sin, we see a devastating combination. When someone spreads lies and slander, the sin multiplies every time those lies spread. Even while they sleep, they accumulate sin as their fabrications continue to harm others - like passive income, except in evil. The liability keeps adding up because they initiated that chain of harm.

translation: Who hears the verses of Allah recited to him, then persists arrogantly as if he had not heard them. So give him tidings of a painful punishment
commentary:
يَسْمَعُ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ثُمَّ يُصِرُّ مُسْتَكْبِرًا كَأَن لَّمْ يَسْمَعْهَا ۖ فَبَشِّرْهُ بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ

He hears the ayat of Allah ﷻ being recited to him, then he turns away in arrogance, insisting on his position, as though he had never even heard them. So give him the good news of painful punishment.

The word يُصِرُّ  carries multiple meanings - to insist on something, and to turn away. This person hears divine revelation and either stubbornly clings to his position out of pride, or dismisses it entirely. The phrase كَأَن لَّمْ يَسْمَعْهَا (as if he never heard it) captures the willful ignorance - he acts as if the message never reached his ears at all. Then comes the bitter irony: فَبَشِّرْهُ (give him good news) - but the "good news" is painful punishment. This is the consequence of deliberate rejection wrapped in arrogance.

translation: And when he knows anything of Our verses, he takes them in ridicule. Those will have a humiliating punishment
commentary:
وَإِذَا عَلِمَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا شَيْـًٔا ٱتَّخَذَهَا هُزُوًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ

And whenever he comes to know anything about Our revelation, he takes it as a joke. Those are the people who will have humiliating punishment.

Sometimes a person hears something from the Quran - maybe on the news, in passing conversation, through media - and their response is to mock it. They find it funny, ridiculous, impossible to take seriously. I've seen videos online where groups of people sit together studying the Quran solely to laugh at it, treating divine revelation as entertainment for their disbelief. When someone mocks what Allah ﷻ has revealed, the punishment is uniquely fitting: عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (humiliating punishment). They sought to humiliate the truth, so they will be humiliated.

translation: Before them is Hell, and what they had earned will not avail them at all nor what they had taken besides Allah as allies. And they will have a great punishment
commentary:
مِّن وَرَآئِهِمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَلَا يُغْنِى عَنْهُم مَّا كَسَبُوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا مَا ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ

Right behind them - though they cannot see it - is Hellfire. And whatever they earned in this life will not benefit them at all, nor will the protectors and guardians they took besides Allah ﷻ. They alone will have tremendous punishment.

The phrase مِّن وَرَآئِهِمْ (from behind them) indicates something they're completely unaware of, something approaching that they cannot see. Hellfire is right there, but they're oblivious. Everything they worked for in this world - their wealth, status, political positions, salaries, credentials - none of it will help them. The allies they relied upon instead of Allah ﷻ will prove utterly useless. They will face عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (tremendous punishment) completely alone.

translation: This [Qur'an] is guidance. And those who have disbelieved in the verses of their Lord will have a painful punishment of foul nature
commentary:
هَـٰذَا هُدًى ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مِّن رِّجْزٍ أَلِيمٍ

This, in fact, is guidance. And those who disbelieved in the miraculous signs of their Master will have punishment made of painful, filthy calamity.

Allah ﷻ declares هَـٰذَا هُدًى (this is guidance). We've encountered different descriptions of the Quran in recent surahs: هُدًى (guidance), ذِكْرٌ (remembrance), and كِتَاب (book). The Quran is guidance for those who accept it. For those who reject the ayat of their own Master - and notice it says رَبِّهِمْ (their Rabb), the One who created and sustains them - their punishment is described as رِّجْزٍ , which can mean debilitating sickness, humiliating illness, or filth. It's painful and degrading all at once.

translation: It is Allah who subjected to you the sea so that ships may sail upon it by His command and that you may seek of His bounty; and perhaps you will be grateful
commentary:
۞ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلْبَحْرَ لِتَجْرِىَ ٱلْفُلْكُ فِيهِ بِأَمْرِهِۦ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ

Allah ﷻ is the One who subdued the ocean for you so that ships can sail through it by His command, and so that you can seek out His favor, and so that you may be grateful.

The word سَخَّرَ  means to subdue, to bring under control, to make something serve a purpose. Allah ﷻ has calmed the mighty oceans, making them navigable. Ships sail بِأَمْرِهِۦ (by His command) - it's only by His permission that massive vessels can traverse vast waters. The purpose? So we can seek His فَضْل (favor) - fishing, trade, travel, exploration of the treasures He's placed in the seas. 

But notice the final purpose: وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (so that you may be grateful). One of the primary reasons the ocean exists in its subdued state is so human beings can express gratitude to Allah ﷻ. The Prophet ﷺ described that when the Day of Judgment arrives and the trumpet is blown, there will be no one left on earth who says "La ilaha illa Allah." When the people of gratitude are gone, the ocean will have no reason to remain calm - because one of its core purposes was to inspire our thankfulness.

translation: And He has subjected to you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth - all from Him. Indeed in that are signs for a people who give thought
commentary:
وَسَخَّرَ لَكُم مَّا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا مِّنْهُ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ

And He has subdued for you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth - all of it, from Him. Surely in that are miraculous signs for a people who engage in deep thought.

Allah ﷻ extends the concept of subjugation beyond the oceans to everything in the skies and earth. جَمِيعًا  - all of it, collectively. Perhaps this hints at future discoveries and technologies that will unlock more of what Allah ﷻ has placed in the universe for us. When Allah ﷻ taught Adam (AS) the names of all things - وَعَلَّمَ ءَادَمَ ٱلْأَسْمَآءَ كُلَّهَا - He didn't teach him the word for airplane or WiFi or computer. But He gave him the foundational tools of language from which all vocabulary could grow. Just as our language is an offspring of Adam's (AS) original language, our knowledge and discovery of what Allah ﷻ has subdued in the heavens and earth continues to unfold. The signs are there لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (for people who reflect deeply).

translation: Say, [O Muhammad], to those who have believed that they [should] forgive those who expect not the days of Allah so that He may recompense a people for what they used to earn
commentary:
قُل لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يَغْفِرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ أَيَّامَ ٱللَّهِ لِيَجْزِىَ قَوْمًۢا بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ

Say to those who believe: they should forgive those who don't hope for the days of Allah ﷻ, so that a people may be compensated for what they earned.

The phrase أَيَّامَ ٱللَّهِ (the days of Allah) refers to the Day of Judgment, the day of death, the ultimate meeting with Allah ﷻ. This ayah came down during the early Makkan period when Muslims faced constant mockery, insults, and sometimes physical abuse. They were told: right now, forgive them. Don't think about vengeance. Don't curse them or pray against them. Just forgive those who have no hope of meeting Allah ﷻ.

This connects back to what we learned in Surah Fussilat: even if there's animosity between you and the one you're calling to the word of Allah ﷻ, treat them as though they're your most intimate friend. This requires extraordinary patience and emotional maturity. Allah ﷻ will handle their recompense - لِيَجْزِىَ قَوْمًۢا بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (so that a people may be compensated for what they earned). That's His job, not ours.

translation: Whoever does a good deed - it is for himself; and whoever does evil - it is against the self. Then to your Lord you will be returned
commentary:
مَنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَنْ أَسَآءَ فَعَلَيْهَا ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ

Whoever acts righteously does so only for the benefit of his own soul. And whoever engages in evil does so only against himself. Then to your Master will all of you be returned.

This is a fundamental principle of divine justice. Every righteous deed benefits only the one who performs it. Every evil deed harms only the one who commits it. When we think we've hurt someone else through our sins, we've actually only hurt ourselves - the primary liability is on us. فَلِنَفْسِهِۦ (for his own self) and فَعَلَيْهَا (against himself) - these pronouns emphasize individual responsibility. Then comes the inevitable return: ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ (then to your Master you will all be returned). Every account will be settled.

translation: And We did certainly give the Children of Israel the Scripture and judgement and prophethood, and We provided them with good things and preferred them over the worlds
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحُكْمَ وَٱلنُّبُوَّةَ وَرَزَقْنَـٰهُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ وَفَضَّلْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

And We certainly gave the Children of Israel the Book, wisdom, and prophethood. And We provided them from good and pure things, and We gave them preference over all other nations.

In the previous surah, we learned that Allah ﷻ had favored the Israelites. Now He explains what those favors entailed. They received الْكِتَـٰبَ (the Book - the Torah), الْحُكْمَ (wisdom and sound judgment), and النُّبُوَّةَ (continuous prophethood). Between Musa (AS) and Isa (AS), there was no break in prophetic guidance. Every time a prophet died, another came. Some scholars estimate this prophetic lineage lasted nearly 1,400 years - constant divine shepherding through messenger after messenger. On top of that, they received provision from pure, good things, and Allah ﷻ gave them preference over all other nations of their time.

translation: And We gave them clear proofs of the matter [of religion]. And they did not differ except after knowledge had come to them - out of jealous animosity between themselves. Indeed, your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ
commentary:
وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُم بَيِّنَـٰتٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ ۖ فَمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوٓا۟ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِى بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ

And We gave them clear proofs regarding the command. Yet they didn't fall into disagreement until after knowledge came to them - out of an urge to rebel and dominate one another. Surely your Master will judge between them on the Day of Judgment regarding everything they differed about.

The word الْأَمْرِ (the command) is one of the Quran's terms for religion and divine guidance. Allah ﷻ gave the Israelites بَيِّنَـٰتٍ (clear proofs, evident signs) about how to live. So why did they fall into division? فَمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوٓا۟ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ (they didn't disagree until after knowledge came to them). Knowledge that should have unified them became the very tool of their division. Why? بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ (out of transgression among themselves) - an urge to dominate, to be superior, to claim authority over others.

This isn't just ancient history about the Israelites. This is a mirror held up to all of humanity, and especially to the Muslim Ummah today. Allah ﷻ wants us to learn more so we become more humble, but human beings often learn more and become more arrogant. People flaunt their credentials - doctor, professor, PhD - not always to serve truth, but to establish superiority. This disease isn't limited to secular knowledge; it infects religious knowledge too.

Within Islam, factions claim they alone possess the correct understanding. Disagreements about moon sighting, prayer methods, or legal opinions become tribal battles. One group claims more followers, another claims purer methodology. Instead of uniting around the Book of Allah ﷻ, communities fracture over which imam to follow or which interpretation to accept. Religious discourse becomes competition - like Toyota and Honda commercials, fighting for market share.

At the individual level, this disease manifests subtly. Someone gains religious knowledge and starts teaching. People appreciate their work and shower them with compliments. They get used to the praise, then addicted to it. When someone mentions another teacher who gave an amazing talk, jealousy creeps in. "Mine wasn't good enough?" The thought festers. Later, when that other teacher's name comes up, they respond, "He's good, but he has some issues. I don't agree with certain things he says." There might be no real disagreement - just wounded pride. If you can't rise higher, bring the other person down. That's بَغْيًۢا (transgression) born from ego.

Someone sits in the audience thinking, "Why wasn't I invited to speak? I know more than him. They're missing out." They wait for the speaker to make a mistake so they can criticize later. This is the pathetic state of turning the highest calling - sharing Allah's word - into a popularity contest. 

When knowledge becomes a means of furthering personal pride and agendas rather than a source of humility and unity, we've fallen into the same trap that destroyed the Israelites. The ayah ends with a sobering reminder: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِى بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ (surely your Master will judge between them on the Day of Judgment). All these petty disagreements, these battles for superiority, these divisions we create - Allah ﷻ will settle them all. The question is: will we be on the side of those who used knowledge to unite, or those who used it to divide?

translation: Then We put you, [O Muhammad], on an ordained way concerning the matter [of religion]; so follow it and do not follow the inclinations of those who do not know
commentary:
**ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَىٰ شَرِيعَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ فَٱتَّبِعْهَا وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ**

After describing how previous communities corrupted their guidance, Allah ﷻ addresses the Prophet ﷺ directly: ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَىٰ شَرِيعَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ - then We established you upon a شَرِيعَة (Sharia) from the command. 

The word شَرِيعَة  deserves careful attention. Its root meaning comes from the image of a path that leads to water. Picture someone traveling through the desert - starving, dehydrated, on the verge of death. Finally, they discover a path, and they can see it leads downward toward water. The journey becomes easier with each step because they know life-saving water awaits them. The verb شَرَعَ is used when you take a camel all the way to water but don't force it to drink. The camel reaches the water but decides for itself when to drink - you cannot compel it.

This is the profound meaning of Sharia. It represents the wisdom, laws, teachings, and guidance from Allah ﷻ - but He doesn't force them upon you. You must make that choice yourself. The same concept appears in Surah Ash-Shura, where we discussed شَرَعَ  in detail.

Over time in Islamic history, the word Sharia became associated primarily with law. Terms like قانون ) and حدود  are specifically legal terms - statutes, restrictions, constitutional law. But Sharia in the Quran is not limited to law alone. Islamic law can be described as Sharia because it opens pathways to mercy, but the Quranic concept of Sharia encompasses far more than legal rulings.

Law essentially consists of three categories: what you must do, what you must not do, and what you're allowed to do. There's also what you should do but aren't in trouble for omitting. For example, prayer must be performed in specific ways at specific times. Certain foods are forbidden. Certain actions are prohibited. Then there are permissible matters - where you can work, what you can eat, how you can conduct business. Finally, there are recommended deeds that elevate your rank as a believer, but their omission doesn't make you a criminal.

However, beyond this legal framework exists an entire realm of moral and spiritual teachings that cannot be codified into measurable law. The Quran commands احسان - excellence and kindness to parents. But how do you quantify that? What specific actions constitute "being the best to your parents"? Your best might differ from mine. It varies across families and cultures. You cannot create a legal checklist that says, "If you don't do these five things, you're not good to your parents." These are moral teachings that live in the heart and manifest in countless individual ways.

Allah ﷻ gave the Prophet ﷺ a beautiful Sharia - a life-saving pathway from the command of the religion. The instruction is clear: فَٱتَّبِعْهَا - follow it. وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ - and don't follow the empty whims of people who don't even know.

When voices call for Muslims to reconsider their teachings to accommodate contemporary pressures, when people who don't understand Sharia demand its rejection, remember this command. The first time I heard someone say, "We're going to ban Sharia law from our state," I wondered: Do they even know what that means? Sharia teaches kindness to parents, care for neighbors, honesty in business, truthfulness in testimony, and justice in all dealings. People speak against what they don't comprehend.

translation: Indeed, they will never avail you against Allah at all. And indeed, the wrongdoers are allies of one another; but Allah is the protector of the righteous
commentary:
**إِنَّهُمْ لَن يُغْنُوا۟ عَنكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ وَلِىُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ**

Allah ﷻ warns the Prophet ﷺ - and through him, all believers - that those who call you away from divine guidance cannot help you in any meaningful way: إِنَّهُمْ لَن يُغْنُوا۟ عَنكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا - they will not benefit you against Allah in any way whatsoever. When you face the consequences of your choices, those who encouraged you to compromise your faith will not come to your aid.

This warning extends even to the Prophet ﷺ himself. Allah ﷻ says in Surah Al-Isra: وَلَوْلَآ أَن ثَبَّتْنَـٰكَ لَقَدْ كِدتَّ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْـًٔا قَلِيلًا - If We had not made you firm, you might have inclined toward them a little bit. And had that happened, إِذًۭا لَّأَذَقْنَـٰكَ ضِعْفَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ وَضِعْفَ ٱلْمَمَاتِ - We would have made you taste double the punishment of life and death. 

If even the Prophet ﷺ would face such consequences for the slightest compromise, what does that mean for us? It teaches that compromise is simply not an option when it comes to the beautiful way Allah ﷻ has taught us.

The ayah continues with a stark observation about the nature of wrongdoers: وَإِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ - oppressors are only protective friends of one another. They support each other in their oppression, finding solidarity in their shared rejection of truth. But وَٱللَّهُ وَلِىُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ - Allah is the protective friend, the guardian, the ally of those who protect themselves through righteousness and God-consciousness.

translation: This [Qur'an] is enlightenment for mankind and guidance and mercy for a people who are certain [in faith]
commentary:
**هَـٰذَا بَصَـٰٓئِرُ لِلنَّاسِ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ**

This ayah beautifully describes what has been revealed: هَـٰذَا بَصَـٰٓئِرُ لِلنَّاسِ - these are clear insights for the benefit of humanity. The word بَصَـٰٓئِر  is the plural of بصيرة , meaning insight, inner vision, enlightenment. This revelation opens the eyes of understanding.

وَهُدًى - and it is guidance, showing the way forward through all of life's complexities. وَرَحْمَةٌ - and it is mercy, for what greater mercy exists than divine guidance that saves you from destruction in this life and the next?

But notice the specific recipients identified: لِّقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ - for a nation that seeks to be convinced, for people who want certainty. The guidance, insight, and mercy are available to all, but only those who genuinely seek conviction will find it. Only those who approach with sincere hearts, wanting to be convinced by truth rather than defending their preconceptions, will benefit from these clear insights.

translation: Or do those who commit evils think We will make them like those who have believed and done righteous deeds - [make them] equal in their life and their death? Evil is that which they judge
commentary:
**أَمْ حَسِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱجْتَرَحُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ أَن نَّجْعَلَهُمْ كَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ سَوَآءً مَّحْيَاهُمْ وَمَمَاتُهُمْ ۚ سَآءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ**

Allah ﷻ now poses a rhetorical question that exposes the absurdity of thinking belief doesn't matter: أَمْ حَسِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱجْتَرَحُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ - Have they assumed, those who have injured themselves with sins? 

The verb اجْتَرَحُوا۟  creates beautiful imagery. In Arabic, جوارح  refers to trained hunting birds - hawks and eagles that ancient Arabs would train to catch prey and bring it back to their owner. The verb carries connotations of earning, but also of injury. Instead of simply saying "those who earned sins," Allah ﷻ says they injured themselves with their sins, like a bird that tears into its prey.

The question continues: Do these people who have injured themselves with sins assume أَن نَّجْعَلَهُمْ كَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ - that We're going to make them equal to those who believed and did righteous deeds? Do they think سَوَآءً مَّحْيَاهُمْ وَمَمَاتُهُمْ - that their lives and deaths will be the same?

Consider the believing young person who guards their faith in an environment hostile to it. They attend college where they're surrounded by temptations at every turn. Their friends tell them they're missing out on the "college experience" - the parties, the relationships without boundaries, the freedom from religious restrictions. Sometimes they even wonder themselves if they're sacrificing too much.

But what are they really giving up? The opportunity to please Allah ﷻ instead of their desires. The chance to build a life of meaning instead of momentary pleasure. They're investing in eternity while others spend everything on a few fleeting years.

Do you think the life of that believing student means the same to Allah ﷻ as the life of someone who lived without any restrictions, doing whatever they wanted whenever they wanted? Do you think when both eventually return to the earth, their lives and deaths carry equal weight?

سَآءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ - What a horrible judgment some people have made! What a terrible decision to assume it's all the same in the end, that belief and disbelief, righteousness and sin, self-control and indulgence will all lead to the same outcome.

translation: And Allah created the heavens and earth in truth and so that every soul may be recompensed for what it has earned, and they will not be wronged
commentary:
**وَخَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلِتُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ**

The answer to the previous question comes swiftly: وَخَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ - Allah created the heavens and the earth with truth, with purpose, with justice. This creation isn't random or meaningless. Everything exists for a reason.

وَلِتُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ - And so that every soul can be recompensed for exactly what it earned. This is why the heavens and earth exist - to provide a realm where choices matter, where actions have consequences, where justice will ultimately prevail. The creation itself points to accountability.

وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ - And they will not be wronged in the slightest. No one will receive less than they deserve or more punishment than they earned. Perfect justice awaits.

translation: Have you seen he who has taken as his god his [own] desire, and Allah has sent him astray due to knowledge and has set a seal upon his hearing and his heart and put over his vision a veil? So who will guide him after Allah? Then will you not be reminded
commentary:
**أَفَرَءَيْتَ مَنِ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَـٰهَهُۥ هَوَىٰهُ وَأَضَلَّهُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ وَخَتَمَ عَلَىٰ سَمْعِهِۦ وَقَلْبِهِۦ وَجَعَلَ عَلَىٰ بَصَرِهِۦ غِشَـٰوَةً فَمَن يَهْدِيهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ**

Allah ﷻ now presents the most dangerous form of shirk: أَفَرَءَيْتَ مَنِ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَـٰهَهُۥ هَوَىٰهُ - Have you seen the one who takes his desires, his whims, his temptations, and turns them into his god?

This describes the narcissist, the person obsessed with themselves. Their most important goal is their own pleasure, their own gratification, their own comfort. Everything revolves around me, me, me. This person worships themselves.

Earlier in this surah, we encountered Pharaoh's claim: أَلَيْسَ لِى مُلْكُ مِصْرَ - "Don't I own the sovereignty of Egypt?" That was shirk in the political sense - claiming the right to decide what is right and wrong for society. But here we see shirk at the personal level. Someone might think, "I have an urge to look at something inappropriate online. I'm going to look because I'm in charge. I pay for my internet connection. I pay for my device. I can watch whatever I want."

This is worship of the self. The god being obeyed is one's own whims. You don't need a physical idol when you've made an idol of your desires. And this idol cannot be destroyed with a hammer - it's embedded deep within the heart. Even heart surgery cannot remove this one.

But notice what makes this particularly tragic: وَأَضَلَّهُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ - Allah let him go astray despite his knowledge. This isn't someone ignorant of their faith. This isn't someone who never studied the Quran or learned about the Prophet ﷺ. No, Allah ﷻ is describing someone who knows everything, who is knowledgeable.

He knows. But still, that greedy self, that نفس أمارة بالسوء (the self that commands toward evil), has such a grip on him that he cannot let it go. When faced with a choice between what Allah ﷻ wants and what he wants, his desires always win. His knowledge becomes useless.

وَخَتَمَ عَلَىٰ سَمْعِهِۦ وَقَلْبِهِۦ - He placed a seal on his hearing and his heart. Notice the sequence here. The hearing is mentioned before the heart, and there's profound wisdom in this order.

When it comes to receiving beneficial counsel and guidance, how does it reach your heart? Through hearing. You hear a lecture, a reminder, a verse of Quran being recited - this enters through your ears and travels to your heart. When the hearing is blocked, how will your heart ever benefit? The pathway is cut off.

But there's another remarkable dimension to this sequencing. This is the 23rd ayah of the surah. If you travel back to the 8th ayah - 15 sentences earlier - you find: يَسْمَعُ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ - He hears Allah's signs being recited to him. The spiritual crime mentioned earlier was one of the ear. And now, 15 ayahs later, the Quran is precise enough to mention sealing the hearing first, then the heart. The crime was committed by the ear, so the ear is sealed first.

What's even more remarkable: these same two elements - heart and hearing - appear in Surah Al-Baqarah, but in reverse order. There Allah ﷻ says: خَتَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَعَلَىٰ سَمْعِهِمْ - Allah sealed their hearts and their hearing. When you examine the beginning of Al-Baqarah, what do you find? ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ لَا رَيْبَ ۛ فِيهِ - This is a Book in which there is no doubt. Where does doubt reside? In the heart. هُدًۭى لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ - Guidance for those who have taqwa. Where does guidance settle? In the heart. ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ - Those who believe in the unseen. Where does belief reside? In the heart. The entire conversation centers on the heart, so the heart is mentioned first when sealing is mentioned.

This represents just one of hundreds of examples of how the Quran sequences its words with impossible precision. How could a human being track such details across dozens of sentences, maintaining thematic consistency while speaking? They simply cannot. This is divine speech.

The ayah continues: وَجَعَلَ عَلَىٰ بَصَرِهِۦ غِشَـٰوَةً - And He placed a cover over his vision. First the hearing, then the heart, then the eyes - all sealed against guidance.

فَمَن يَهْدِيهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱللَّهِ - Who will guide him after Allah? Once Allah ﷻ has placed these locks on someone's ears, heart, and eyes, who can possibly guide them? أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ - Will you not then take counsel? Will you not remember? Will you not make an effort to think?

translation: And they say, "There is not but our worldly life; we die and live, and nothing destroys us except time." And they have of that no knowledge; they are only assuming
commentary:
**وَقَالُوا۟ مَا هِىَ إِلَّا حَيَاتُنَا ٱلدُّنْيَا نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيَا وَمَا يُهْلِكُنَآ إِلَّا ٱلدَّهْرُ ۚ وَمَا لَهُم بِذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ ۖ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَظُنُّونَ**

This ayah presents the Quran's commentary on hardcore materialism - the philosophy that denies any unseen reality beyond the physical world.

وَقَالُوا۟ مَا هِىَ إِلَّا حَيَاتُنَا ٱلدُّنْيَا - They said, "There is nothing except this worldly life of ours." According to this worldview, there's no such thing as a soul, no angels, no heaven or hell. We're merely atoms and cells. Physical existence is all there is.

نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيَا - We die and we live. Notice that death is mentioned before life. Why? Because for the materialist, death dominates their thinking. They're depressed about mortality. Yes, we live a little while, but ultimately we're going to die. Since death is inevitable and final, we might as well pursue maximum pleasure while we're here. We might as well fulfill every whim and desire we have. You only live once, as the saying goes - the classic materialist motto.

وَمَا يُهْلِكُنَآ إِلَّا ٱلدَّهْرُ - Nothing destroys us except time. They don't say "Allah causes us to die." They attribute everything to the passage of time, to natural processes, to the inevitable march of the cosmos. Time is their only god.

But then comes the devastating response: وَمَا لَهُم بِذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ - They have no knowledge about this whatsoever. Their entire worldview, their confident assertions about the nature of existence, their denial of the unseen - none of it is based on actual knowledge.

إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَظُنُّونَ - They are doing nothing except supposing, assuming, guessing. They've built their entire lives on speculation dressed up as certainty. They've made themselves slaves to assumptions they cannot prove, rejecting guidance they refuse to examine.

translation: And when Our verses are recited to them as clear evidences, their argument is only that they say, "Bring [back] our forefathers, if you should be truthful
commentary:
وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ مَّا كَانَ حُجَّتَهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ ٱئْتُوا۟ بِـَٔابَآئِنَآ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

When Allah's miraculous signs—clear and self-evident—are recited to them, they have no intellectual response. مَا كَانَ حُجَّتَهُمْ إِلَّا—the only case they have left, the only refutation they can bring forward is to mockingly demand: "Bring forward our ancestors, the ones who have already passed away, if you're telling the truth!"

This is the bankruptcy of their argument. When confronted with clear evidence, with logical proofs, with signs in creation itself, they resort to sarcasm rather than reason. It's not a genuine request for evidence—it's a deflection, a way to avoid engaging with the truth being presented to them.

This same challenge appeared in the previous surah, showing it's a recurring tactic of those who deny resurrection. They think they're being clever, but they're actually revealing the weakness of their position. When you have no logical counterargument, you resort to mockery.

translation: Say, "Allah causes you to live, then causes you to die; then He will assemble you for the Day of Resurrection, about which there is no doubt, but most of the people do not know
commentary:
قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يُحْيِيكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يَجْمَعُكُمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

Allah ﷻ commands His Messenger ﷺ to respond with clarity: "Tell them—forget about your ancestors for a moment. Let's talk about you. Allah ﷻ gives you life, then He gives you death, then He will gather you to the Day of Standing."

The response is profound in its directness. They're asking about bringing back the dead as if it's impossible, but Allah ﷻ redirects them to examine their own existence. You are already evidence of His power to give life. You will experience His power to take life. Why would gathering you again be beyond His capability?

لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ—there is no doubt whatsoever in that Day. The certainty is absolute. Yet وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ—most people don't have a clue. They don't know, they don't understand, despite all the evidence around them and within themselves.

translation: And to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth. And the Day the Hour appears - that Day the falsifiers will lose
commentary:
وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَخْسَرُ ٱلْمُبْطِلُونَ

To Allah ﷻ alone belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. This establishes His absolute authority and power—the One who owns and controls all of creation can certainly bring about resurrection.

وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ—on the day when the Hour is established, when the Day of Judgment begins to unfold, when the trumpet is blown and all chaos breaks loose on the earth—يَوْمَئِذٍ يَخْسَرُ ٱلْمُبْطِلُونَ—that is the day when those who nullified religion, who called it a lie, who rejected the truth, will find themselves utterly bankrupt.

The word المُبْطِلُونَ  refers to those who declared truth to be false, who made the real unreal. On that Day, their greatest loss will be revealed—not in material terms, but in the ultimate currency of existence itself.

translation: And you will see every nation kneeling [from fear]. Every nation will be called to its record [and told], "Today you will be recompensed for what you used to do
commentary:
وَتَرَىٰ كُلَّ أُمَّةٍ جَاثِيَةً ۚ كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ تُدْعَىٰٓ إِلَىٰ كِتَـٰبِهَا ٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

This is one of the most powerful images in the Quran, and it's why this entire surah bears the name Al-Jathiyah. You will see every nation that has ever existed جَاثِيَةً—brought to its knees, unable to stand, utterly humbled before Allah ﷻ.

The word جَاثِيَةً  comes from the root جَثَا , meaning to fall on one's knees in humility or from exhaustion. Every civilization, every people that ever walked the earth—regardless of their power, their pride, their accomplishments—will be on their knees that Day.

كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ تُدْعَىٰ إِلَىٰ كِتَـٰبِهَا—every nation will be called to its book. "Weren't you the nation that followed Isa (AS)? Where's your book? Weren't you the people of Musa (AS)? Where's your book? What book did you follow?"

الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ—today you will be compensated fully for the things you used to do. Complete accountability, complete justice.

translation: This, Our record, speaks about you in truth. Indeed, We were having transcribed whatever you used to do
commentary:
هَـٰذَا كِتَـٰبُنَا يَنطِقُ عَلَيْكُم بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنسِخُ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

Allah ﷻ speaks to all of humanity gathered in this gigantic assembly: "This is Our book"—not their book, but Allah's book. It speaks against them بِٱلْحَقِّ (with truth), with absolute accuracy and purpose.

Then comes the remarkable word: نَسْتَنسِخُ . The root نَسَخَ  means to copy, to document, to create a certified duplicate. In Arabic, استنسخ  carries the meaning of making something firm, confirming it, creating a backup copy.

إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنسِخُ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ—We have always kept certified copies of everything you used to do. This speaks to preservation beyond the individual record each person receives. Even if someone tries to tear up their book, to destroy the evidence, Allah ﷻ says: We have certifiable copies that will be read to you. There's no escaping the record of your deeds.

translation: So as for those who believed and did righteous deeds, their Lord will admit them into His mercy. That is what is the clear attainment
commentary:
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَيُدْخِلُهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْمُبِينُ

Now the verdicts begin. First, those who believed and did the righteous deeds expected of them. Notice it doesn't say Allah ﷻ will enter them into Paradise—it says فَيُدْخِلُهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦ—their Master will enter them into His mercy.

This is profoundly meaningful. Paradise itself is manifestation of Allah's mercy. Even the righteous deeds we perform are only possible through His mercy—the guidance to do them, the ability to do them, the acceptance of them. It's mercy upon mercy upon mercy.

ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْمُبِينُ—that is the ultimate success, the clear victory, the obvious triumph. Everything else people consider success in this world pales in comparison to entering Allah's mercy on that Day.

translation: But as for those who disbelieved, [it will be said], "Were not Our verses recited to you, but you were arrogant and became a people of criminals
commentary:
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَفَلَمْ تَكُنْ ءَايَـٰتِى تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ وَكُنتُمْ قَوْمًا مُّجْرِمِينَ

As for those who disbelieved, listen to what will be said to them. Allah ﷻ Himself addresses them: "Were My miraculous revelations not being recited to you?"

The question is rhetorical—of course they were. The ayat came to them, clear and compelling. But فَٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ—you responded with arrogance. You became great in your own eyes. The استكبار —seeking to be great, rejecting truth out of pride—this is what prevented them from accepting guidance.

وَكُنتُمْ قَوْمًا مُّجْرِمِينَ—you remained a criminal nation. The word كُنتُمْ indicates a continuous state. It wasn't a momentary lapse; you persisted as a people who committed crimes against the truth, against your own souls, against Allah's signs.

translation: And when it was said, 'Indeed, the promise of Allah is truth and the Hour [is coming] - no doubt about it,' you said, 'We know not what is the Hour. We assume only assumption, and we are not convinced
commentary:
وَإِذَا قِيلَ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَٱلسَّاعَةُ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهَا قُلْتُم مَّا نَدْرِى مَا ٱلسَّاعَةُ إِن نَّظُنُّ إِلَّا ظَنًّا وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُسْتَيْقِنِينَ

The ayah continues with their response when reminded of the Day of Judgment: "Whenever it was said that Allah's promise is true, and the Hour—there's no doubt in it—you used to say: 'We have no clue what Hour you're talking about. We're just making assumptions. We're not convinced.'"

This is the ayah of agnosticism. The previous ayah (24) dealt with materialists who definitively deny. This deals with those who claim uncertainty as their shield.

مَا نَدْرِى مَا ٱلسَّاعَةُ—"We don't know." إِن نَّظُنُّ إِلَّا ظَنًّا—"We're just making assumptions, guesses. We can't be sure of anything." وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُسْتَيْقِنِينَ—"And we're not convinced, we're not gradually heading towards any kind of certainty."

The word مُسْتَيْقِنِينَ  is significant—it refers to someone actively seeking certainty, moving toward conviction. These people aren't even looking. It's not that they're on a journey to find truth; they've made peace with not knowing and not seeking to know.

"I don't know if there's a God or not. Nobody came back from the dead to tell me. God hasn't shown up to talk to me personally, so I can't be sure He's around. And I'm not necessarily interested in finding out either." This attitude echoes the arrogance of Pharaoh who told Haman to build him a tower so he could climb up and confront Musa's God—because "I'm not convinced."

translation: And the evil consequences of what they did will appear to them, and they will be enveloped by what they used to ridicule
commentary:
وَبَدَا لَهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتُ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ

The evil of what they had done will بَدَا —appear before them, manifest, take material form. Everything they dismissed, every sin they trivialized, every truth they laughed at—it becomes visible, undeniable reality.

وَحَاقَ بِهِم—it surrounds them, takes hold of them, envelops them completely. What surrounds them? مَا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ—the very thing they used to mock, the very reality they made fun of. The judgment they joked about, the Hellfire they dismissed as fairy tales—it's all there, and they're in the middle of it.

translation: And it will be said, "Today We will forget you as you forgot the meeting of this Day of yours, and your refuge is the Fire, and for you there are no helpers
commentary:
وَقِيلَ ٱلْيَوْمَ نَنسَىٰكُمْ كَمَا نَسِيتُمْ لِقَآءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هَـٰذَا وَمَأْوَىٰكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ

It will be said to them: "Today We are ignoring you, deliberately forgetting you, dismissing you—كَمَا نَسِيتُمْ لِقَآءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هَـٰذَا—just as you dismissed, forgot, ignored the meeting of this Day of yours."

This is measure for measure. They treated the Day of Judgment as irrelevant, not worth their attention, not worth preparing for. Now they receive the same treatment—divine inattention, being dismissed, cast aside.

وَمَأْوَىٰكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ—and the place for you to return to, to remain in, is the Fire. وَمَا لَكُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ—you will have no helpers whatsoever, no aid, no one to rescue you or relieve your situation.

translation: That is because you took the verses of Allah in ridicule, and worldly life deluded you." So that Day they will not be removed from it, nor will they be asked to appease [Allah]
commentary:
ذَٰلِكُم بِأَنَّكُمُ ٱتَّخَذْتُمْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ هُزُوًا وَغَرَّتْكُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا يُخْرَجُونَ مِنْهَا وَلَا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ

"That punishment is because you took Allah's miracles, His signs, His revelations—you took them هُزُوًا—as a joke, as mockery, as entertainment."

وَغَرَّتْكُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا—and worldly life deceived you, deluded you, conned you. It made its temporary pleasures look so attractive that you forgot the permanent reality awaiting you.

فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا يُخْرَجُونَ مِنْهَا—that Day, they will not be brought out of it, out of the Fire. There's no exit, no escape.

وَلَا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ—and they will not be given any relief or respite. The word استعتاب  comes from تَعَب , meaning exhaustion. استعتاب means to remove someone's exhaustion, to give them rest.

Think of coming home tired and asking someone to massage your head—that's isti'tab, removing your fatigue. Or a boxer between rounds, getting water sprayed on him, muscles massaged, preparing for the next round. Even in torture chambers, torturers give victims breaks—not out of mercy, but so they don't die and can feel the pain again when torture resumes.

But in Hellfire, there is no isti'tab. The punishment is continuous. You don't pass out from the pain. There's no break, no moment of relief, no time to recover. It just continues without cessation.

translation: Then, to Allah belongs [all] praise - Lord of the heavens and Lord of the earth, Lord of the worlds
commentary:
فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْحَمْدُ رَبِّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَرَبِّ ٱلْأَرْضِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

The surah concludes powerfully: فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْحَمْدُ—to Allah alone, praise and gratitude belong. Notice it's not just الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ (praise belongs to Allah), but فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْحَمْدُ—the structure emphasizes exclusivity.

رَبِّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَرَبِّ ٱلْأَرْضِ—Master of the heavens and Master of the earth, رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ—Master of all nations, all worlds, all peoples.

There's something remarkable here. At the beginning of the Quran, in Surah Al-Fatihah, Allah ﷻ says الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ—praise belongs to Allah. But here at the end of this surah, He says فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْحَمْدُ—adding the emphasis of "only" or "exclusively."

Why the difference? The word "only" is used when you're refuting someone, when you need to correct a wrong assumption. If someone says, "I wasn't the only one talking," she's actually saying more than just those words—she's implying her friend should go down with her too.

When you say "praise only belongs to Allah," you're inherently saying it doesn't belong to anyone else. You're correcting those who direct praise elsewhere.

An idol worshiper doesn't necessarily object to "praise belongs to Allah"—he can agree with that while still praising his idols alongside Allah. But he definitely objects to "praise only belongs to Allah" because that excludes his false deities.

This surah dealt extensively with the arrogant, with those obsessed with themselves, with those who took gods besides Allah ﷻ. If you simply told them "praise belongs to Allah" at the conclusion, they'd say, "Yeah, sure, okay—and to our gods too." That's why the correction comes with emphasis: لِلَّهِ—to Allah exclusively, and no one else.

translation: And to Him belongs [all] grandeur within the heavens and the earth, and He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise
commentary:
وَلَهُ ٱلْكِبْرِيَآءُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

To Him alone belongs الْكِبْرِيَآءُ—greatness, grandeur, magnificence—in the heavens and the earth.

Why does Allah ﷻ mention His greatness specifically at the end of this surah? He didn't mention this quality at the end of Al-Fatihah. The reason becomes clear when you consider the surah's themes: it dealt repeatedly with people who are مُسْتَكْبِرِينَ —those seeking to be great, the arrogant ones who wanted greatness for themselves.

After addressing them ayah after ayah, after showing their false claims and ultimate humiliation, Allah ﷻ declares at the conclusion: Greatness? That's not for you. That belongs exclusively to Me. وَلَهُ ٱلْكِبْرِيَآءُ—to Him alone is all magnificence.

وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ—and He is the Ultimate Authority, full of wisdom. The Mighty who cannot be overcome, the Wise whose every decree and judgment contains perfect wisdom.

This powerful conclusion brings together the surah's central themes: the exclusive right of Allah ﷻ to praise, to greatness, to authority—and the ultimate accountability that awaits all who denied these realities.
